question_title: How many confirmed Ebola (Bundibugyo virus) deaths will the WHO report in the DRC as of its latest update near June 28, 2026?
summary:
This question asks for the cumulative confirmed DRC Ebola deaths in the latest WHO DON published by June 27, 2026. The most recent DON (DON607, June 13) reported 136 deaths as of June 10. MoH data as of June 14 shows 192 deaths with 808 confirmed cases. Death growth has been ~9%/day (June 10-14) though case growth is already decelerating from ~7% to ~4.5%/day. No vaccine or treatment exists for Bundibugyo virus.

The resolution hinges on (1) how many more DONs are published before June 27 and their data cutoff dates, and (2) the death trajectory. Monte Carlo simulation (200K runs) mixing exponential decay, case-based CFR, and sustained growth models, combined with probabilistic DON timing, gives a median of ~290. About 80% of simulations have DON609 qualifying (data cutoff ~June 22-25), while ~20% have only DON608 (cutoff ~June 16-18). The distribution is moderately right-skewed reflecting tail risk from sustained rapid growth in a response-overwhelmed setting.

factors.description: Outbreak actively growing with no vaccine/treatment - deaths doubled from 91 to 192 in 8 days (June 6-14). No licensed countermeasure for BDBV means outbreak continues.

Case growth rate already decelerating from ~7%/day to ~4.5%/day (June 6-14). Death growth will follow case deceleration with a lag. International response scaling up (IASC activation, $518M response plan).

DON publication timing uncertainty: ~80% chance DON609 qualifies (data cutoff ~June 22-25, deaths ~290-400), ~20% chance only DON608 (cutoff ~June 16-18, deaths ~230-260). This is the primary source of bimodality.
Response struggling: MSF says outbreak 'outpacing response', Oxfam says scale likely underestimated, 72% contact tracing rate, conflict environment. Supports sustained higher growth.
Rising CFR from 17.7% to 23.8% (June 6-14) increases deaths even if case growth slows. CFR likely stabilizes around 25-27%.

reasoning:
## Forecast Summary

**Question:** How many confirmed Ebola (Bundibugyo virus) deaths will the WHO report in the DRC as of its latest update near June 28, 2026?

**Median estimate: 290 confirmed deaths**

### Key Distribution Points
| Percentile | Deaths |
|-----------|--------|
| P10 | 236 |
| P25 | 257 |
| P50 (median) | 290 |
| P75 | 340 |
| P90 | 399 |

### Key Reasoning

1. **Current trajectory:** Deaths doubled from 91 (June 6) to 192 (June 14) — an 8-day doubling time with no licensed vaccine or treatment available for Bundibugyo virus.

2. **Growth deceleration signal:** Case growth has already slowed from ~7%/day to ~4.5%/day, and death growth should follow with a lag, suggesting the exponential phase is beginning to moderate.

3. **DON timing is the biggest uncertainty:** The resolution depends on which WHO DON is the latest published by June 27. ~80% of simulations have DON609 qualifying (data cutoff ~June 22-25, higher death counts), while ~20% have only DON608 (cutoff ~June 16-18, lower counts).

4. **Response challenges persist:** No vaccine/treatment, conflict environment, low contact tracing rates (72%), and organizations reporting the outbreak is "outpacing the response."

5. **Monte Carlo simulation** mixed 4 growth models (exponential with deceleration, case-based with CFR, sustained high growth, rapid response) across 200K runs with probabilistic DON timing to produce the final distribution.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) How many confirmed Ebola (Bundibugyo virus) deaths will the WHO report in the DRC as of its latest update near June 28, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/44088)
[Ebola disease caused by Bundibugyo virus, Democratic Republic of the Congo & Uganda](https://www.who.int/emergencies/disease-outbreak-news/item/2026-DON607)
[Ebola disease outbreak in the Democratic Republic of the Congo and Uganda](https://www.ecdc.europa.eu/en/ebola-outbreak-democratic-republic-congo-and-uganda)
resolution_criteria: This question asks for the cumulative number of confirmed deaths in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC only, excluding Uganda) attributed to the ongoing Ebola disease (Bundibugyo virus) outbreak, as reported in the most recent World Health Organization Disease Outbreak News (DON) article on this outbreak published on or before June 27, 2026. The relevant DON articles are listed at https://www.who.int/emergencies/disease-outbreak-news (filter by 2026 / Ebola). Report the figure as the integer count of confirmed deaths (e.g., a report of 158 confirmed deaths resolves as 158). The "confirmed deaths" figure for DRC is the number of deaths among laboratory-confirmed cases stated for the DRC in that DON.
fine_print: "Confirmed deaths" refers to deaths among laboratory-confirmed cases, not deaths among suspected or probable cases; if the DON reports a combined "confirmed and probable" death tally for DRC alongside a confirmed-only figure, use the confirmed-only figure. Only the DRC figure counts; Uganda or any other country's deaths are excluded, and any "combined" cross-country total is not used. If multiple qualifying DON articles are published on or before June 27, 2026, use the most recently published one. If, as of June 27, 2026, no DON article on this outbreak has been published later than the 6 June 2026 DON, the question resolves to the DRC confirmed-death figure from that most recent available DON (91). If the WHO DON page is unavailable, the same figure may be taken from the corresponding WHO situation report or from [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions) reporting the WHO DON figure. If the outbreak is officially declared over and WHO reports a final DRC confirmed-death total, that final figure is used.

factors.description: Outbreak actively growing with no vaccine/treatment - deaths were 91 on June 6 and 192 on June 14 (doubling in 8 days). No licensed countermeasure for BDBV means outbreak will continue.
Case growth rate already decelerating from ~7%/day to ~4.5%/day (June 6-14). Death growth will follow case deceleration with lag. International response scaling up (IASC, $518M response plan).

DON publication timing uncertainty: most likely 2 more DONs before June 27 (DON608 ~June 18-20, DON609 ~June 24-27), but the data cutoff is 2-3 days before publication. If only DON608 qualifies, deaths much lower (~230-260) vs DON609 (~290-400).

Response struggling: MSF says outbreak 'outpacing response', Oxfam says scale likely underestimated, conflict environment, poor water/sanitation, low contact tracing rate (72%). These factors suggest death growth remains high.
Rising CFR from 17.7% to 23.8% over June 6-14 increases deaths even if case growth slows. CFR likely stabilizes around 25-27%.

input.anchor: No strong prior anchor for this specific question type. Starting from the trajectory extrapolation: deaths doubling roughly every 7-8 days from 192 on June 14 would give ~384 by June 22, but deceleration is expected.
input.assessment:
This is a rapidly growing Ebola outbreak with no vaccine/treatment. The question resolves to the confirmed DRC death count in the latest WHO DON published by June 27. The latest DON (DON607, June 13) reported 136 deaths as of June 10. MoH data as of June 14 shows 192 deaths. We expect 1-2 more DONs before the deadline.

The key tension is between:
1. Continued rapid death growth (~8-9%/day exponential) which would push deaths to 300-400+ by the DON609 data cutoff
2. Evidence that case growth is already decelerating (from 7% to 4.5%/day), which death growth will follow with a lag
3. DON publication timing: ~80% chance DON609 qualifies (data cutoff ~June 22-25), ~20% chance only DON608 (cutoff ~June 16-18)

Monte Carlo simulation (200K runs) mixing multiple growth models gives median ~290, with P10=236 and P90=399. The distribution is right-skewed reflecting the possibility of sustained high growth.
input.calibration_notes: The lower bound of 91 is the fallback to DON607 if no new DON is published, which is extremely unlikely given the PHEIC status and weekly DON pattern. The simulation gives essentially 0% probability of this. Distribution seems well-calibrated: P10=236 corresponds to a scenario where only DON608 qualifies with a June 16-17 cutoff, P90=399 corresponds to DON609 with sustained high growth.
input.key_uncertainties:
1. DON publication timing and data cutoff dates are the largest source of uncertainty
2. Whether death growth rate has already started declining (lagging the observed case deceleration)
3. Potential for response to dramatically slow transmission vs outbreak overwhelming response capacity
input.tool_audit: research() - Excellent, returned comprehensive outbreak data with timeline and sources. web_search - Useful for finding latest DON and ECDC data. fetch_url - Successfully extracted key data from WHO DON607 and ECDC pages. execute_code - Essential for Monte Carlo simulation and growth analysis.
input.process_reflection: The research tool worked well for this outbreak question, pulling from ReliefWeb and WHO sources systematically. The main friction was inability to directly check the WHO DON listing page (it returned only introductory text). The Monte Carlo framework handles the compound uncertainty (growth rate × DON timing × data cutoff) well. A dedicated epidemic modeling tool would help - something that could fit SIR/SEIR models to case data automatically.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Verification Summary

**Hallucinated evidence:** None found. All key factual claims in the 5 factors are grounded in the research trace:
- Factor 1: Deaths 91 (June 6) and 192 (June 14) confirmed via WHO DON607 and ECDC/MoH data in trace. No licensed countermeasure for BDBV confirmed in question background and multiple trace sources.
- Factor 2: Case growth deceleration from ~7%/day to ~4.5%/day confirmed by agent's own computation in trace (June 6-10: 7.0%/day, June 10-14: 4.6%/day). $518M response plan confirmed via Africa CDC/WHO joint plan in search results.
- Factor 3: DON publication timing model based on documented DON602 (May 17), DON605 (May 29), DON606 (June 8), DON607 (June 13) pattern from trace. Simulation correctly shows ~79% DON609 qualifying, ~21% DON608 only.
- Factor 4: MSF "outpacing response" quote, Oxfam "underestimated" claim, and 72% contact tracing rate all confirmed by ReliefWeb sources in trace and worldview research entry.
- Factor 5: CFR progression from 17.7% (DON606/June 6) to 20.1% (DON607/June 10) to 23.8% (192/808, June 14) confirmed in trace.

**Double-counting:** Minor overlap between Factor 1 (growth trajectory) and Factor 4 (response quality), as both push toward higher death estimates. However, they draw on distinct evidence — Factor 1 uses quantitative growth data while Factor 4 uses qualitative response assessments from different organizations. Not materially problematic for a numeric forecast.

**Wrong-direction factors:** None. All factor logits are positive and all central estimates (275-340) are above the current known death count (192), which is directionally correct for projecting forward.

**Contradictory assessment:** No. The assessment's center of 290 is consistent with the factor-implied average (~307) and the Monte Carlo simulation median (290).

**Resolution criteria alignment:** Excellent. The agent correctly identified that resolution depends on the DRC confirmed-death figure in the latest WHO DON published on or before June 27, modeled DON publication timing probabilistically, and accounted for the data cutoff lag (2-3 days before publication).

**Counterargument quality:** Strong. The counterargument identifies specific evidence (case growth deceleration as a leading indicator, DON609 timing uncertainty) and constructs a coherent alternative scenario that would yield a lower median (~250-280). This is a genuine challenge to the forecast, not a token gesture.

**Confidence calibration:** The 0.40 confidence is appropriate given dual sources of uncertainty (growth trajectory AND DON publication timing). No overconfidence issue.

**Probability assessment:** The center of 290 with range 236-399 appears well-calibrated. The agent ran 200K Monte Carlo simulations mixing four growth models (moderate deceleration 35%, case-based 30%, sustained high growth 20%, rapid deceleration 15%), validated against known data points, and produced results that are internally consistent. The sensitivity analysis further confirms the range is reasonable across different growth assumptions and DON cutoff dates.

**Tool audit:** No discrepancies between the narrative tool audit and actual tool metrics. All tools reported 0% error rates, consistent with the agent's characterization.
